md: When you have a list of numbers — test scores, daily temperatures, household incomes — a **measure of center** is a single number that represents 'where the data sits.' Three measures matter: **mean**, **median**, and **mode**. Each gives a slightly different summary, and each can mislead in characteristic ways.
The **mean** (often just 'average') is the sum of all values divided by the count.

blocks.title: The mean is sensitive to outliers
blocks.body: Add one extreme value and the mean shifts a lot. If the same class had a sixth student score $20$, the mean drops from $84$ to $\dfrac{420 + 20}{6} \approx 73.3$ — a five-point drop from a single score.

md: **Sort** the data, then the **median** is the middle value.
**Even $n$:** the average of the two middle entries.
**The median ignores extremes.** Add a value of $1000$ to either example and the median barely moves — only one middle position changes.
The **mode** is the value (or values) that appears most often.
A dataset can have **one mode** (unimodal), **multiple modes** (bimodal, multimodal), or **no mode** (every value is unique).

blocks.title: Choosing wisely
blocks.body: **Mean:** best for symmetric, outlier-free data. Easy to combine and compute. **Median:** best when data is skewed or has outliers (e.g., income, house prices). **Mode:** best for categorical data ('most popular color') or when a peak matters.

md: ### Worked example — find a missing value
The mean of five numbers is $20$. Four of them are $10, 18, 22, 30$. Find the fifth.
A course grade is 30% homework, 30% midterm, 40% final. A student earned: HW $90$, midterm $70$, final $85$. Course grade?
**Weighted mean:** multiply each value by its weight, then sum.
## 6. See mean, median, and mode side by side
Pick a distribution shape in the widget below. Watch how the mean (blue), median (red), and mode(s) (green) shift as the shape changes from symmetric, to skewed, to bimodal, to having an outlier.

blocks.title: Watch as you flip presets
blocks.body:
**Symmetric** → all three markers cluster near the center.

**Right-skewed** → the mean shifts RIGHT of the median, dragged by the tail.

**With outlier** → the mean leaps in the direction of the outlier; the median barely budges.

**Bimodal** → the mean lands between the two peaks, at a value no observation actually has — a clear signal that 'mean' alone hides the structure.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Mean** = sum / count. Sensitive to outliers. **Median** = middle (sorted). Robust. **Mode** = most frequent. Good for categories. For skewed data, the median often tells the truer story.
